title: A Ballad of Burdens by Algernon Charles Swinburne — Sad Poetry Lines
content:
The burden of fair women. Vain delight,
And love self-slain in some sweet shameful way,
And sorrowful old age that comes by night
As a thief comes that has no heart by day,
And change that finds fair cheeks and leaves them grey,
And weariness that keeps awake for hire,
And grief that says what pleasure used to say;
This is the end of every man's desire.
meaning: "A Ballad of Burdens" by Algernon Charles Swinburne is a sad and love and deep and nature poem consisting of 87 lines. The poet explores themes of sad and love and deep and nature through vivid imagery and emotional depth. Beginning with "The burden of fair women. Vain delight, And love self-slain in some sweet shameful way,...", this piece captures the essence of human experience and invites reflection on the deeper currents of life and feeling. This work is part of Algernon Charles Swinburne's celebrated body of poetry that continues to resonate with readers worldwide.
